prompt: Determine whether this installation should pass inspection. Identify the visible defects, classify the risk, and list the immediate corrective actions.
scenario: A residential garage subpanel is shown with the deadfront removed during rough inspection. The feeder includes separate insulated neutral and bare equipment grounding conductors. The neutral bar is bonded to the cabinet with the green bonding screw installed. Several bare equipment grounding conductors and white neutral conductors terminate on the same bar. One 20 A breaker has two branch-circuit conductors under a single terminal. An unused knockout opening is visible on the lower right side of the enclosure. Working clearance is otherwise clear.
title: Electrical panel makeup: subpanel defects

prompt: Assess whether the drain configuration is acceptable and explain the likely failure mode.
scenario: A bathroom vanity drain drops vertically out of the sink tailpiece into a P-trap, then immediately turns downward through the cabinet floor with no horizontal trap arm before the vent connection. There is no visible cleanout. The homeowner says the trap holds water after the faucet runs but occasionally gurgles when the tub drains.
title: DWV trap and venting: vanity S-trap

prompt: Identify the safety and compliance issues and state whether the system should remain in service.
scenario: A gas furnace is installed in a closet that opens to an attached garage. The return-air grille is cut into the garage-facing wall. The single-wall metal vent connector passes within about one inch of exposed wood framing. The condensate tube from the coil runs upward for several inches before dropping to a floor drain.
title: Furnace installation: venting and return-air hazards

prompt: Decide if this connection can be accepted and list what evidence or rework is needed.
scenario: A beam splice detail calls for pretensioned A325 bolts. The field photo shows several bolts with head markings that do not match the submitted bolt grade. No turn-of-nut match marks or DTI washer indications are visible. The inspector notes the crew described the joint as 'snug tight only' because the connection looked stable.
title: Structural bolting: pretension evidence

prompt: Assess readiness for concrete placement, identify the defects, and estimate the stage of the work.
scenario: A pre-pour photo shows a slab mat with reinforcing bars resting directly on the vapor barrier and soil in multiple areas. Chairs are missing along the near edge, and several bars are touching the side form. The placement drawing requires continuous bottom cover and lap splices are otherwise visible.
title: Rebar placement: cover and support

prompt: Evaluate the LOTO sequence and state the minimum corrections before work can continue.
scenario: A technician is preparing to service a conveyor. The disconnecting breaker is switched off and held with red tape but has no lock. A paper tag is taped to the panel door. The work order checklist skips verification of zero energy and does not mention stored mechanical energy in the raised conveyor section.
title: Lockout/tagout: taped breaker and no try step

prompt: Decide if the repair passes quality control and list the defects that matter.
scenario: After a front brake job, the left rotor measures 23.4 mm with a stamped minimum thickness of 24.0 mm. The caliper hose makes a half twist between the hard line and caliper when the wheels are straight. The pads are new and the pedal feels firm during a short parking-lot test.
title: Brake service: rotor and hose defects

prompt: Evaluate whether the high-voltage work can proceed and identify the required safety steps.
scenario: An EV battery service photo shows the orange service disconnect removed and placed on the cowl. The technician immediately begins removing the inverter cover. The class 0 gloves in use have a test date that expired nine months ago. No meter verification of absence of voltage is shown.
title: EV high-voltage safety: incomplete isolation

prompt: Assess the installation quality and name the defects.
scenario: A basement supply branch uses flexible duct with visible sags between hangers. Several metal-to-flex joints are wrapped only with gray cloth duct tape; no mastic, UL-listed foil tape, or mechanical draw bands are visible. The homeowner says air comes out of the register, but the room is always cold.
title: Duct installation: flex sag and unsealed joints

prompt: Determine whether this joint is ready for test and what must be corrected.
scenario: A process piping flange is shown before hydrotest. Two adjacent bolt holes are empty. The installed bolts have uneven thread projection, and no torque witness marks are visible. The gasket tag indicates the correct material for the service.
title: Flanged joint: missing bolts and torque evidence

prompt: Evaluate the sequence and explain the likely problem before final acceptance.
scenario: A pump and motor were laser-aligned while the suction and discharge piping were still disconnected. After piping was bolted up, the coupling gap changed visibly and the laser report was not repeated. The crew wants to grout the base and install the coupling guard.
title: Shaft alignment: sequence and pipe strain

prompt: Identify the hazards and state whether work may continue.
scenario: Workers are installing a sewer lateral in an approximately 6-foot-deep trench. The trench walls are nearly vertical with no trench box, shoring, or sloping. Spoil is piled within one foot of the edge. No ladder is visible within the photographed work area.
title: Trench protection: missing protective system

prompt: Determine whether this installation should pass inspection. Cite the specific observations that support your decision and note any conditions.
scenario: A residential garage subpanel is shown with the deadfront removed at rough inspection. The feeder lands on separate neutral and equipment grounding bars; the green bonding screw is absent and stored in the parts bag taped inside the cabinet. Every breaker terminal holds a single conductor. All knockout openings are filled. A typed panel schedule is affixed to the door and working clearance in front of the panel is unobstructed.
title: Subpanel makeup inspection (residential job)

prompt: Assess this joint against the bolted-joint assembly requirements. Cite the observations supporting your decision.
scenario: A 6-inch Class 150 flanged joint is inspected after bolt-up. All eight studs are installed with hardened washers, torque witness lines are marked across every nut, and stud ends project one to three threads past each nut. The gasket visible at the joint edge matches the spiral-wound type called out on the line class sheet. The torque record sheet shows a completed star-pattern sequence in three passes.
title: Flanged joint closure inspection (industrial site)

prompt: Evaluate whether the documented procedure was performed correctly and in the right order.
scenario: A line-set replacement is documented with photos: a nitrogen regulator connected and flowing during each braze, Schrader cores removed and set aside before evacuation, and a micron gauge at the far port reading 320 microns. The tech's log shows the system isolated from the pump and holding below 500 microns for fifteen minutes before charging began.
title: Brazing and evacuation review (service call)

prompt: Determine whether this connection meets the specified pretension requirements. Cite the visible evidence.
scenario: A moment connection specified as pretensioned is inspected. Bolt heads show F3125 Grade A325 markings matching the drawing callout. Every DTI washer's silicone squirts are fully extruded at all bumps. Match marks made at snug-tight show consistent nut rotation across the connection. No thread run-out sits in the shear plane at the visible bolts.
title: Structural bolting inspection (commercial project)

prompt: Verify the measurements against specification and determine whether this work is acceptable for release.
scenario: A completed front brake job is documented. The micrometer reads 28.4 mm on a rotor stamped MIN TH 26.0 mm. New pads are seated with hardware and shims, caliper guide pins show fresh silicone lube on the boots, and the work order records lug nuts torqued to 103 ft-lb in a star pattern with a torque wrench pictured on the wheel. Road test noted normal pedal.
title: Brake service check (service call)

prompt: Identify any hazards requiring immediate control, or confirm the protective system is adequate as observed.
scenario: A 6-foot-deep utility trench has workers inside a trench shield whose certification plate and tabulated data are visible. Shield walls extend above grade, the spoil pile sits roughly four feet back from the edge, and an extension ladder inside the shield extends three rungs above grade about ten feet from the workers. A competent-person daily inspection log dated today is shown.
title: Trench protection review (commercial project)

prompt: Evaluate whether the alignment procedure was sequenced and completed correctly.
scenario: A pump-motor alignment record shows soft foot checked and corrected to 0.001 inch at each foot, final laser alignment performed after the suction and discharge flanges were bolted up, and the as-left readings within the 1800-rpm tolerance table posted on the job card. Cold offsets for hot service were applied per the OEM growth chart.
title: Shaft alignment review (industrial site)

prompt: Inspect the fall protection setup as observed and identify any deficiencies, or confirm it is acceptable.
scenario: A worker at 15 feet on a steel deck wears a full-body harness with clean, unfrayed webbing; the fold-out impact indicator tag is intact and the inspection grid is punched for the current quarter. The lanyard snaps to an engineered anchor strap around a structural beam marked with a rated-anchor tag, connected at shoulder height.
title: Fall protection review (commercial project)

prompt: Determine whether this termination can be accepted from the evidence provided. If not, state exactly what verification is required.
scenario: A photo shows feeder lugs in a disconnect, conductors fully seated with correct strip length and no visible strand damage. Nothing in the photo or the provided paperwork indicates whether the lugs were torqued to the marked values, and no torque marks or record sheet is included.
title: Feeder termination acceptance (commercial project)

prompt: Can this drain arrangement be approved from what is shown? State what additional evidence would settle it.
scenario: Under a bathroom sink, a P-trap with correct outlet geometry connects to a sanitary tee entering the wall. The wall is closed and finished. No photo of the rough-in stage and no vent information for this fixture group is provided.
title: Drain vent path assessment (residential job)

prompt: Assess whether the quoted repair is justified by the evidence provided. State what testing should occur before parts are ordered.
scenario: A scan report shows stored code P0420 with a freeze frame captured at steady 45 mph cruise. The service writer has quoted a catalytic converter replacement. No upstream or downstream oxygen sensor waveform data, fuel trim values, or exhaust leak inspection results are attached to the ticket.
title: Catalyst efficiency code diagnosis (service call)

prompt: Determine whether entry can be authorized on this permit as documented. Identify exactly what is missing.
scenario: A confined-space entry permit for a process vessel shows an oxygen reading of 20.9 percent logged thirty minutes ago. The LEL, H2S, and CO fields on the permit are blank, and the meter's bump-test record for today is not attached. The attendant and rescue plan sections are complete.
title: Confined-space atmosphere review (industrial site)

prompt: Can bar size compliance be confirmed from this evidence? State what measurement or reference is needed.
scenario: A pre-pour photo of a grade beam shows bars on chairs with consistent spacing and clear cover to the forms. The photo is taken from too far away to read bar deformation patterns or count ribs, and the placing drawings specify different bar sizes for the top and bottom mats. No scale reference is in frame.
title: Rebar size verification (commercial project)

prompt: Classify this condition and determine whether it represents a defect requiring corrective construction work.
scenario: A one-year warranty walkthrough of a non-fire-rated interior partition notes three small circular bumps in the paint film along one stud line, consistent with seasonal lumber shrinkage. Fastener spacing on the original inspection photos met the schedule, and the wall shows no cracking, movement, or moisture staining.
title: Drywall screw pops assessment (residential job)

prompt: Assess this observation and recommend an appropriate disposition for the customer.
scenario: During an oil change on a 140,000-mile vehicle, the tech photographs slight dampness with dust accumulation along the lower edge of the valve cover gasket. There are no drips on the undertray or the ground, oil level is at the full mark, and the customer reports no oil consumption between changes.
title: Valve cover seep assessment (service call)

prompt: Identify this condition and determine whether corrective masonry work is warranted.
scenario: Three months after completion, a new CMU veneer shows a light white powdery haze across several courses after a wet winter. Joints are tooled concave and intact, weep holes at the bottom course are open and unobstructed, and interior humidity readings behind the wall are normal.
title: Masonry efflorescence assessment (commercial project)

prompt: Give the inspection decision, then explain the difference between what the code permits here and what experienced electricians typically prefer, and why.
scenario: A living-room circuit uses receptacles wired via push-in backstab connections with 14 AWG solid copper on a 15 A circuit, consistent with the device listing. The homeowner asks whether the work must be redone. Local amendments add nothing beyond the model code.
title: Backstabbed receptacles decision (residential job)

prompt: Judge whether releasing this vehicle was acceptable. Weigh the absence of warning lights against the OEM requirement, and state the correct disposition.
scenario: A windshield with a forward-facing camera bracket was replaced. The glass installer road-tested the vehicle, confirmed no dash warnings, and released it, noting the shop lacks calibration targets and the customer declined a dealer visit. The OEM procedure for this model specifies a static target calibration after glass replacement.
title: ADAS calibration decision (service call)

prompt: Judge the operator's reasoning against the specification. State the correct disposition and the cost-versus-risk tradeoff involved.
scenario: During a line-side rework, an operator reinstalls the same stretch bolts that were removed an hour earlier, reasoning that they were only torqued once and the replacement kit would stop the line for twenty minutes. The engineering spec marks these fasteners single-use with an angle-tightening step.
title: Torque-to-yield bolt reuse decision (production floor)

prompt: Make the go/no-go call. Weigh the schedule pressure explicitly and state the conditions under which the lift could proceed.
scenario: A vessel lift calculates to 92 percent of chart capacity at the working radius. Site wind is averaging 18 mph with gusts to 28 mph against a manufacturer wind limit of 20 mph for this boom configuration. The schedule shows the crane demobilizes tomorrow and the next window costs a week of standby.
title: Crane pick in gusty wind decision (industrial site)

prompt: Judge whether this tie-off is acceptable for a five-minute task. Address the difference between guardrail strength and anchorage requirements.
scenario: A worker leaning over a roof edge to fasten trim has clipped a lanyard to the top rail of a temporary guardrail, explaining that the nearest engineered anchor is forty feet away and the task takes five minutes. The guardrail is built to standard guardrail strength requirements, not personal fall arrest anchorage ratings.
title: Guardrail anchor decision (commercial project)

prompt: Read the measurement and the stamped minimum from the image. Report the measured thickness in the `value` field (mm) and decide whether the rotor may remain in service.
scenario: The image shows a digital micrometer reading taken on a front brake rotor and the specification stamp on the rotor hat.
title: Measurement check: Brakes (photo, service call)

scenario: The image shows micron gauge readings at pump isolation and ten minutes later during a standing decay test on an evacuated refrigeration system.
title: Fault diagnosis: Brazing & evacuation (photo, service call)

prompt: Listen to the idle cadence. Classify the condition from the rhythm, identify the likely fault pattern if one is present, and give the disposition.

title: Fault diagnosis: Timing & mechanical (audio, service call)

title: Fault diagnosis: Bearings & seals (audio, production floor)
